coredata.dc:title: The effects of Se/S ratio on the photoelectric properties of nitrogen -doped graphene quantum dots decorated CdSxSe1-x composites
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Yun
Du, Beibei
Du, Peng
Wu, Yuncui
Wang, Yongqin
Li, Can
Luo, Linhui
Zou, Bingsuo
coredata.dc:description: In the work, CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites were fabricated via a simple one-step hydrothermal process and their tunable composition, structure and photoelectric properties were characterized by various techniques. The photoelectric properties of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs could be adjusted by different Se/S ratios and tunable band-gaps. CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites reached the optimal photocurrent response and the lowest interfacial impedance at the Se/S ratio of 0.75:0.25. Mott-Schottky plots and LSV spectra showed that the n-type CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs presented a higher carrier density under light illumination. The excellent properties of the composites could be attributed to the mechanism involved in the excitation and electron-transfer process. On one hand, the band-gap of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs was narrowed, and more electrons were excited by the lower band-gap energy to promote a superior electron separation and transportation. On the other hand, N-GQDs acted as charge carriers and conductive way providers for electron-transfer instead of electron-hole recombination in the composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdSxSe1−x
N-GQDs
Tunable photoelectric properties
Bandgap narrowing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical analysis of the N-GQDs
FT-IR analysis of N-GQDs and CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites
I-t and LSV analysis of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image (a) and height distribution (b) of N-GQDs.

PL spectra of N-GQDs (a), PLE spectrum of N-GQDs (b) at the detection wavelength of 440 nm, UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs (c) and the energy band diagram of the N-GQDs (top) along with associated absorption, PLE, and PL spectra at 320 nm (bottom) (d).
XRD patterns of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs(a) and enlarged view of the XRD pattern (b).
Zeta potential of CdS0.25Se0.75, GQDs and CdS0.25Se0.75/GQDs composites.
Full scan XPS spectrum of the CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d (b), S 2p (c), Se 3d (d), C 1s (e)and O 1s (f).
FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs and CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and Tauc spectra (b) of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites.
Time-dependent photocurrents curves of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites (a) and LSV curves of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites (b) in dark and light of 365 nm.
Nyquist plots of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites in dark (a), in light (b) and Nyquist plots of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites (c) in dark and light of 365 nm.
Mott-Schottky plots of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites in dark and light of 365 nm.
Schematic illustration of the tunable ternary CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites.

head.ce:title: The effects of Se/S ratio on the photoelectric properties of nitrogen -doped graphene quantum dots decorated CdSxSe1-x composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, Peoples R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of resources and environmental engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan, Hubei, 430070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of resources and environmental engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the work, CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites were fabricated via a simple one-step hydrothermal process and their tunable composition, structure and photoelectric properties were characterized by various techniques. The photoelectric properties of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs could be adjusted by different Se/S ratios and tunable band-gaps. CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites reached the optimal photocurrent response and the lowest interfacial impedance at the Se/S ratio of 0.75:0.25. Mott-Schottky plots and LSV spectra showed that the n-type CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs presented a higher carrier density under light illumination. The excellent properties of the composites could be attributed to the mechanism involved in the excitation and electron-transfer process. On one hand, the band-gap of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs was narrowed, and more electrons were excited by the lower band-gap energy to promote a superior electron separation and transportation. On the other hand, N-GQDs acted as charge carriers and conductive way providers for electron-transfer instead of electron-hole recombination in the composites.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, alloy semiconductors have been widely applied in various fields including optical, electronic, and catalytic applications [1–5]. Therein, CdSxSe1−x (0 < x < 1) is a type of important II–VI ternary semiconductors with a tunable band-gap (1.7–2.4 eV), almost covering the entire visible spectral range [6–8]. Song et al. employed the in-situ hydrothermal method to prepare CdSxSe1-x quantum dots with adjustable band-gap as photoanodes for solar cell [9]. Thi et al. presented that the optical band-gap of CdSxSe1-x quantum dots varied linearly with the composition [10]. Dai et al. investigated the effect of composition-tunable ternary CdSxSe1−x nanowires on the responsivity of photon sensors [11]. However, despite many advantages, the low transmission efficiency and the fast recombination rate of photoelectrons are still the major problems which limit their practical application, so the introduction of high-electron carriers is a feasible strategy [12,13].

Carbon-based materials are highly desirable for use as electron-transfer carriers [14]. The electronic structure and catalytic activity of 2D carbon-based materials can be tuned by controlling edges, defects, and surfaces [15,16]. Some studies showed that carbon-based materials such as g-C3N4 can be co-doped with other substances as catalysts [17,18]. Different from g-C3N4 in a 2D structure, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a 0D nanomaterial, has been applied in biosensors [19], photodetectors [20], solar batteries [21] and clean energy [22] owing to the special features. Currently, some studies have shown that the insertion of heteroatom into carbon structure could effectively adjust the characteristic properties of GQDs [23]. Compared with other doping elements, nitrogen has the similar atomic radius as carbon. Moreover, the electronegativity of N (χN = 3.04) is larger than that of C (χC = 2.55), so nitrogen can enter the carbon network through substitution doping [22]. The electronic characteristics of GQDs can also be intrinsically altered by n-doping, resulting in an increase in the activity of pristine GQDs for specific reactions and catalysis [24]. Many studies have reported on recombining N-GQDs with semiconductors to accelerate electron migration and reduce the probability of electron-hole recombination, such as N-GQDs/ZnO [25], N-GQDs/In2O3 [26], N-GQDs/NiCo2S4 [27], Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs [28] and so on. Although there were some works on single CdSSe alloy or N-GQDs, literature integrating ternary alloy and N-GQDs was scarcely reported, and the effect of the tunable band-gap on the photoelectric properties of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites was barely discussed.

Herein, CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs with various Se/S ratios were fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal process. The composites were investigated by XRD、TEM、XPS、I-T、EIS、Mott-Schottky and so on. The effects of Se/S ratio on the photoelectric properties of N-GQDs decorated CdSxSe1-x composites were studied in detail. The CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites had tunable photoelectric properties under different Se/S ratios. In addition, N-GQDs can serve as electron conductors to enhance the photoelectric performances of the composites.

Sodium selenite (Na2SeO3), cadmium acetate (Cd-(CH3COO)2), thiourea (H2NCSNH2), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), ammonia hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 28wt%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), nitric acid (HNO3), flake graphite (99.9%), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) were of analytical grade. All preparation processes were provided with deionized water.

Normally, 60 mg of graphite oxide (GO) were dissolved in 120 mL of deionized water. After the ultrasonic dispersion for 30 minutes, 8 mL of hydrogen peroxide and 2.4 mL of ammonia and were quickly added in the above GO solution. Then the solution was autoclaved at 180 ℃ for 6 h. Finally, the supernatant of the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm disposable needle filter.

The composites were prepared using Cd-(CH3COO)2 as the cadmium source, H2NCSNH2 as the sulfur source, Na2SeO3 as the selenium source, and N2H4·H2O as the reducing agent [29]. Firstly, three sources were dissolved with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Subsequently, 3 mL of N-GQDs and 3 mL of N2H4·H2O were added in the solution and the mixed solution was autoclaved at 180 °C for 8 h. After the mixture was washed several times, the final product was obtained by desiccation at 60 °C for 8 h. According to the different amounts of S source and Se source, CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs, CdS0.5Se0.5/N-GQDs and CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs complexes were obtained (Scheme 1 ).

The structural information was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/MAX-RB). The morphological characteristics were observed using transmission electron and atomic force microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F and AFM, Bruker Multimode 8). Elemental analysis was performed on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The optical properties were studied by UV–visible and photoluminescence spectra (UV-5500 and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer). Various groups were investigated by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet, IS-10). The photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance and Mott-Schottky were recorded using electrochemical work station (CHI650e) in a three-electrode system with 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte.

The AFM morphology of N-GQDs in Fig. 1 (a) presented that N-GQDs were dispersed homogeneously. The height distribution of N-GQDs in Fig. 1(b) exhibited that the vertical distance of 2–3 nm corresponded to approximate 4–6 graphene layers of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical analysis of the N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 380 nm, PL spectra appeared a maximum at 320 nm in Fig. 2 (a). Concurrently, the PL peaks exhibited a red-shift, which was attributed to the strong electron affinity of the N atoms in N-GQDs [30]. There was a peak around 315 nm corresponding to the intrinsic luminescence of N-GQDs and a peak around 385 nm indexed to the surface states [31] in Fig. 2(b). As presented in Fig. 2(c), an absorption peak at 210 nm was indexed to the C=C bond and a peak at 305 nm was related to the C=O bond. A new energy level was provided with N doping, which was generated by the Cπ–Nπ* transition relating to C–N and C=N bonds at 375 nm and 417 nm [32]. According to the above spectra, the doping of N elements introduced additional ‘defect sites’ to create new electronic transition pathways in the energy band structure of GQDs. As presented in Fig. 2(d), the new energy levels (Nπ*) were near the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of GQDs. As the energy exceeded 3.31 eV (375 nm), the electrons were excited to promote the Cπ–Nπ* electron transition. Subsequently, the excited electrons were back to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) after vibration relaxation, resulting in the corresponding photoluminescence behavior [33,34].

The XRD patterns of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs、CdS0.5Se0.5/N-GQDs and CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 3 . For comparison, the standard XRD patterns of hexagonal CdSe (JCPDS NO.080459) and CdS (JCPDS NO.751545) were also provided. For ternary CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs, the diffraction peaks were situated between hexagonal CdSe and hexagonal CdS, suggesting that the synthesized CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites also had a hexagonal phase structure. And the peaks of CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs at 2θ of 24.7°, 26.35°, 28.00°, 36.33°, 43.33°, 47.32° and 51.38° were ascribed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) phase of hexagonal CdS0.75Se0.25 (JCPDS NO. 49–1459). The results suggested the formation of homogeneous alloy rather than a physical mixture of CdS and CdSe. As can be seen from the enlargement of the XRD pattern (Fig. 3 (b)), with the increase of Se/S ratio, the diffraction peak gradually shifted to a smaller angle. This phenomenon was since the ionic radius of Se was larger than that of S, which led to an increase in lattice parameters [35]. The diffraction peaks of N-GQDs were absent in the XRD patterns of the CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs attributed to the small amount of N-GQDs.

The TEM images of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs were represented in Fig. 4 . CdS0.25Se0.75/GQDs composites were nanorods with the magnitude of 15–20 nm. As shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), the N-GQDs were attached to CdS0.25Se0.75 and the composites exhibited high resolution lattice fringes. The fringe of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (002) facet of CdS0.25Se0.75, and those of 0.24 nm and 0.34 nm corresponded to the (100) and (002) facets of N-GQDs, respectively [36]. This result demonstrated that the CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites were prepared successfully.

The surface charges of CdS0.25Se0.75, N-GQDs and CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites were analyzed by zeta potential test in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 5 , CdS0.25Se0.75 nanoparticles presented a positive potential of 4.65 mV due to the presence of Cd2+ on the surface, while N-GQDs showed a negative potential of −20.3 mV due to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups. The incorporation of positive CdS0.25Se0.75 with negative N-GQDs caused an obvious change in the zeta potential from 4.65 mV to −1.84 mV, indicating that CdS0.25Se0.75 and N-GQDs were aligned by electrostatic attraction in composites [37].

Fig. 6 showed the elements analysis of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites by XPS measurement. A full spectrum in Fig. 6(a) contained Cd 3d, S 2p, Se 3d, C 1s and O 1s, which was originated from CdS0.25Se0.75 and N-GQDs. The Cd 3d spectrum (Fig. 6(b)) including the binding energy of 405.85 eV and 412.57 eV could be ascribed to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at the high binding energy of the Cd2+ component belonged to surface Cd atoms [38]. The S 2p high-resolution spectrum was decomposed into two components including 160.57 eV and 165.46 eV (Fig. 6(c)) [39]. Fig. 6(d) presented Se 3d peaks of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs, and two fitted peaks at 52.7 eV and 54.88 eV were ascribed to Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2 [40]. In C 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 6(e)), there were three fitted peaks containing 284.05 eV, 285.53 eV and 288.5 eV attributed to sp2 hybridized C–C, sp3 hybridized C=N and C–O, respectively [41]. As shown in O 1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 6(f)), three peaks consisted of 530.05 eV, 531.57 eV, and 532.91 eV corresponded to O–C, O=C and O–H, respectively [42]. In addition, no obvious N 1s peak was found from the XPS spectrum, which was attributed to the small amount of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis of N-GQDs and CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 exhibited the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs、CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs、CdS0.5Se0.5/N-GQDs and CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs composites. All samples presented obvious vibrations at 3446 cm-1 and 1384 cm-1 due to O–H from H2O in air. Small absorption peaks at 2923 cm-1 and 2849 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the C–H bond [43,44]. The wavenumbers around 1014 cm-1 and 1631 cm-1 were indexed to C–O–C and C=O bonds, respectively. In particular, the vibrations at 1400 cm-1 and 3120 cm-1 were ascribed to C–N and N–H bonds in N-GQDs [45]. The FT-IR spectra of the composites did not contain C–N and N–H peaks due to the low content of doped quantum dots, which was confirmed by XPS analysis.

To investigate the optical behavior of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites, UV–vis absorption and Tauc spectra were presented in Fig. 8 . The absorption peaks of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs、CdS0.5Se0.5/N-GQDs and CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs composites (Fig. 8(a)) were located at 511 nm, 502 nm and 487 nm, respectively. As the ratio of Se/S increased, the red-shift of the absorption edge gradually appeared. This demonstrated that the tunable band-gap stemmed from stoichiometry variation in the ternary alloy CdSxSe1−x. The curves in Fig. 8(b) exhibited a slight red-shift since the band-gap energy decreased as S ions were gradually substituted by Se ions in CdSxSe1-x nanoparticles [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: I-t and LSV analysis of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 (a) compared the photocurrent-time responses of CdSxSe1-x/GQDs composites with different Se/S ratios. The responses were sharply increased when UV light was on, and gradually dropped when the light was turned off. The photocurrent responses of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs were adjusted by the ratio of Se/S. Obviously, CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs exhibited an optimal photocurrent of 3.0 × 10-6 A/cm-2, which was nearly twice as large as that of CdS0.75Se0.25/N-GQDs. This result was ascribed to the reason that the tunable band-gap was narrowed at the Se/S ratio of 0.75:0.25. Since the band-gap energy was decreased, more electrons were excited under light irradiation, and more photogenerated electrons were separated and transferred to increase the photocurrent density of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites [47]. As shown in linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) spectra of Fig. 9(b), in comparison to dark conditions, CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites displayed a higher current intensity under the light of 365 nm. The output current (I = I light – I dark) of the CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites increased with the raise of the bias voltage, suggesting a superior electron–hole separation [48].

The interfacial electron-transfer impedance was investigated at an applied bias voltage of 400 mV. As shown in Fig. 10 (a) and (b), the impedance decreased as the Se/S ratio increased, and it reached the smallest at the Se/S ratio of 0.75:0.25. Under light and dark conditions, the charge-transfer resistance of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs achieved the minimum due to the narrowed band-gap identified from UV–Vis and I-t analysis. The equivalent electrical circuit was presented in the illustration of Fig. 10 (c). Rw represented the Warburg impedance, CPE indicated the double-layer capacitance and Rs showed electrolyte resistance, respectively. Rct displayed the charge-transfer resistance associated with the semicircular arc in the high-frequency region. By comparing the Nyquist plots of the CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites in the light and dark fields, the impedance of the composites in light was smaller than that in dark. As the electrons were motivated, the lower electron-transfer resistance promoted more electrons to move faster and produce a higher current density under light irradiation [48,49].

The Mott-Schottky curves were measured with a frequency of 100–2000 Hz and a voltage window of −1 to 1 V. Fig. 11 showed a comparison of the current density and the flat band-gap in dark and light. A positive slope was presented in Mott-Schottky plots, suggesting the n-type semiconductor of the composites. According to equation (1), the donor density was calculated from these positive slopes [50]:
(1)
1
C 2
=
2
ε
ε 0
A 2
eN D
( V −
V fb
−
K B
T
e
)
where A and C indicated the area and capacitance of the electrode. ε and εo were the dielectric constant and permittivity of free space. ND represented the carrier density (the slope of the linear graph of 1/C2 and V), e and T showed the electronic charge and the absolute temperature, KB represented the Boltzmann’s constant, V indicated the applied bias and Vfb was the flat band-gap (the intercept on the V axis).

According to the curves, the fitting slope of the composites in light was smaller than that in dark. Under light irradiation, the carrier density was increased on the surface of the composites according to the above formula. The flat band potential of the composites in light presented a positive shift compared to that in dark due to the change of the charged state of the electrode surface [51,52].

In view of the photoelectric test results, a possible reaction mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 12 . The Se/S ratio in the composites varied from 0.25:0.75 to 0.75:0.25, and the band gap became gradually narrower with increasing Se content, so more photogenerated electrons were separated and transferred, resulting in the maximum current density of the CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs composites. Under UV light, the photogenerated electrons generated on the conduction band of CdSxSe1-x can be easily extracted and transferred to N-GQDs, which provides a new transport path to reduce the electron-hole recombination caused by vibration relaxation. Then the photogenerated electrons stored in the N-GQDs can be spontaneously transferred to the FTO glass and rapidly transferred to the external circuit. Therefore, N-GQDs as electron carriers could reduce electron-hole recombination, increase charge-transfer efficiency, and improve the photoelectric performances of the composites [53].

In summary, a simple one-step hydrothermal method was chosen to fabricate CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs. The effect of Se/S ratio on the properties of composites was studied and the results showed that CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs composites belonged to the alloy rather than independent CdSe and CdS. The photocurrent response of CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs reached an optimal value of 3.0 × 10-6 A/cm-2, which was obvious higher than that of CdSxSe1-x/N-GQDs at other Se/S ratios. Meanwhile, the electrochemical impedance achieved the minimum at the Se/S ratio of 0.25:0.75. Mott-Schottky plots showed that the slope of n-type CdS0.25Se0.75/N-GQDs in light was smaller than that in dark due to the larger carrier density of the composites under light illumination. The above results are ascribed to the relatively small band-gap of CdS0.25Se0.75 and the doping of N-GQDs, which promote the electron transport and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51204129) and open foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Composition-dependent ultra-high photoconductivity in ternary CdSxSe1−x nanobelts as measured by optical pump-terahertz probe spectroscopy
Nano Res

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, J. Lu, M. Zheng, S.H. Tang, X. Zhang, C.H. Sow, Composition-dependent ultra-high photoconductivity in ternary CdSxSe1−x nanobelts as measured by optical pump-terahertz probe spectroscopy, Nano Res. 6 (2013) 808-821. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-013-0359.
